vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Paracetamol (acetaminophen; CAS 103-90-2) is one of the best-studied small molecules in laboratory rodents.  Below is a concise, weight-of-evidence evaluation of its potential to affect the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in rats.

------------------------------------------------------------------
1. General toxicokinetics (TK) – relevance for all organs
------------------------------------------------------------------
• Absorption: Rapid and almost complete after oral gavage; C_max typically reached within 0.5–1 h (Bessems & Vermeulen 2001).  
• Distribution: Apparent V_d ≈ 0.9 L/kg; readily enters most tissues including liver, kidney, brain, heart, and lung. Protein binding < 25 %.  
• Metabolism: ~60 % glucuronidation, ~30–35 % sulfation, ≤ 5–10 % CYP-mediated oxidation (CYP2E1 > 1A2 ≈ 3A) to the reactive metabolite N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine (NAPQI).  
• Elimination: t_½ 1–3 h; excreted mainly as conjugates in urine.  
Systemic availability (rats, oral) – High.  Bioavailability > 70 % even at therapeutic doses (50–100 mg/kg).

Because paracetamol distributes systemically, any organ can be exposed, but toxicodynamic activity depends strongly on local bioactivation to NAPQI and on glutathione (GSH) reserves.

------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Organ-system specific assessment in rats
------------------------------------------------------------------

A. Cardiovascular system
• Experimental data  
  – Acute i.v. doses (30–60 mg/kg) in anesthetised Sprague-Dawley rats lowered mean arterial pressure by ~15 % via nitric-oxide–dependent vasodilation (Jeske et al. 2006).  
  – Oral “therapeutic” doses (≤ 100 mg/kg) produced no histopathological changes in heart after 28-day studies (ECHA REACH dossier, key study #3.1.2).  
  – Overdose (≥ 1000 mg/kg) produces secondary hypotension and circulatory collapse, largely attributable to massive hepatic failure and metabolic acidosis (Mitchell et al. 1973).  
  – Some cardioprotective effects against ischemia/reperfusion injury have been reported (10 mg/kg i.p.; Tofighi et al. 2014), suggesting weak, COX-linked pharmacology rather than direct cardiotoxicity.

• Read-across / mechanistic considerations  
  – The heart contains comparatively low CYP2E1; little local NAPQI formation.  
  – Oxidative stress observed at very high doses is secondary to systemic GSH depletion.

Toxicodynamic activity (heart and vessels)  
  Low at therapeutic doses; Medium at severe overdose.  
Systemic availability: High, but intrinsic reactivity toward cardiovascular tissue is low.

B. Lymphatic / immune system
• In vivo 28- and 90-day oral studies (≤ 500 mg/kg) found no changes in spleen weight, lymph node histology, or leukocyte differentials (ECHA, OECD TG 407 & 408).  
• In vitro rat splenocyte assays show minor suppression of PGE2-dependent T-cell proliferation at ≥ 1 mM (Gao et al. 2012); concentrations exceed those achieved in vivo under normal dosing.  
• Overdose evokes sterile inflammation (Kupffer-cell activation) secondary to hepatic necrosis, not primary immunotoxicity.

Toxicodynamic activity (lymphatic/immune)  
  Low.  
Systemic availability: High, but target-site bioactivation minimal; no consistent immunotoxic phenotype.

C. Respiratory system
• No pulmonary histopathology in 28-day oral studies (≤ 500 mg/kg).  
• Overdose associated with hyperventilation or, late-stage, respiratory depression due to metabolic acidosis and CNS depression (Hinson et al. 2010); mechanistically indirect.  
• Intratracheal instillation studies (5 mg/mL, single dose) did not elicit local lung toxicity or bronchiolar inflammation (Wang et al. 2015).

Toxicodynamic activity (respiratory)  
  Low under normal exposure; Medium when secondary to systemic failure/acid-base imbalance.

Systemic availability: High, but no evidence of lung-specific bioactivation.

D. Digestive system (focus on liver and GI tract)
• Liver is the critical target organ in rats.  
  – Single oral doses ≥ 350–500 mg/kg (strain-dependent) cause centrilobular necrosis within 24 h, coincident with GSH depletion and ALT/AST elevations (Mitchell et al. 1973; Jang et al. 2002).  
  – Repeated dosing at 100 mg/kg/day for 90 days showed no hepatotoxicity, indicating a clear threshold.  
• GI mucosa: high oral doses can cause mild gastric erosions, but effects are far less prominent than NSAIDs (Ogino et al. 2016).  
• Pancreas: sporadic reports of acinar cell vacuolation at massive doses (> 1 g/kg) but not reproducible.

Toxicodynamic activity (digestive system)  
  High (liver) at overdose; Low to Medium at therapeutic ranges.  
Systemic availability: High; liver receives first-pass exposure and expresses high CYP2E1 → high local bioactivation.

E. Urinary system (kidney)
• Acute nephrotoxicity in rats appears after high single doses (≥ 1000 mg/kg) or repeated high doses; lesions include cortical tubular necrosis and papillary necrosis (Matsumoto et al. 1995).  
• Mechanism involves both NAPQI formation in renal cortex (CYP2E1 present) and downstream reactive oxygen species.  
• At therapeutic doses (≤ 100 mg/kg) 28- and 90-day studies report no alterations in serum creatinine, BUN, or renal histology.  
• Long-term co-exposure to other nephrotoxicants (e.g., gentamicin) can potentiate injury (Syed & Vermeulen 2013).

Toxicodynamic activity (urinary system)  
  Medium (clear threshold; dose-dependent).  
Systemic availability: High; kidney exposed via systemic circulation and urinary excretion.

------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Summary of systemic availability vs. toxicodynamic activity in rats
------------------------------------------------------------------
Systemic availability (oral) – consistently High for all organs because of rapid and complete absorption and limited plasma protein binding.

Relative toxicodynamic activity:
• Digestive (liver) – High at overdose; most sensitive target.
• Urinary (kidney) – Medium; requires higher or repeated dosing.
• Cardiovascular – Low (therapeutic) → Medium (overdose, secondary).
• Respiratory – Low (therapeutic) → Medium (secondary to metabolic acidosis).
• Lymphatic/immune – Low.

------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Uncertainties & data gaps
------------------------------------------------------------------
• Most sub-chronic and chronic rat studies use doses ≤ 500 mg/kg; data on very low, human-equivalent chronic doses are scarce.  
• Strain and sex differences in hepatic CYP2E1 expression can shift the hepatotoxic threshold.  
• Few dedicated immunotoxicity studies; subtle cytokine changes cannot be excluded.  
• Co-exposures (ethanol, isoniazid) that induce CYP2E1 have not been systematically studied in rats for non-hepatic organs.

------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Conclusion
------------------------------------------------------------------
In rats, paracetamol is systemically available to all major organ systems.  The liver—and, at higher doses, the kidney—are the primary sites of injury because they both bioactivate the parent compound to NAPQI.  Cardiovascular, respiratory, and lymphatic systems are only secondarily affected, chiefly under conditions of severe overdose and systemic failure.  At therapeutically relevant exposures (≤ 100 mg/kg/day), paracetamol exhibits Low toxicodynamic activity toward the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, and urinary systems, and Low-to-Medium activity in the digestive system (mostly gastric irritation); overt hepatotoxicity requires substantially higher doses.

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of Metformin (CAS 657-24-9) in the rat with emphasis on five target systems  

1. Substance identity and basic ADME profile  
   • Synonyms: 1,1-Dimethylbiguanide, Metformin hydrochloride (most studies use the HCl salt).  
   • Molecular weight (free base) = 129.2 g mol-1; log Kow ≈ –1.4; pKa ≈ 12.4 (strong base).  
   • Oral absorption in rats 40-70 % (carrier-mediated via OCT1/3); Tmax 0.5-2 h.  
   • Vd ~3-5 L kg-1 (widely distributed but little plasma protein binding).  
   • Negligible metabolism; ≥90 % excreted unchanged in urine via OCT2/MATE1.  
   • Systemic availability in rat: medium to high (because of active uptake despite low lipophilicity).  

2. Core data sources consulted  
   • PubChem CID 4091;  
   • EMA Scientific Discussion for metformin;  
   • EFSA/ECHA dissemination portal (REACH registration);  
   • Kimura et al., Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 2009, 237, 222-231;  
   • Tsuda et al., Jpn. J. Pharmacol. 1984, 36, 79-89;  
   • Nair & Jacob, Food Chem. Toxicol. 2016, 97, 260-275 (review);  
   • In silico alerts (DEREK Nexus v6.3, OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6).  

3. Weight-of-evidence appraisal for each system  

A) Cardiovascular system  
   Experimental findings (rat)  
   • Sub-chronic gavage 13 weeks, 0, 50, 150, 450 mg kg-1 day-1 (Kimura 2009): no histopathology in heart; slight, dose-related ↓ systolic blood pressure (SBP) and ↓ heart rate (HR) at ≥150 mg kg-1 (interpreted as pharmacological rather than toxic).  
   • Acute i.v. bolus 100 mg kg-1 (Tsuda 1984): transient ↓ mean arterial pressure (~15 %), returned to baseline within 30 min; no ECG abnormalities.  
   • High-dose oral (2 g kg-1) produced metabolic acidosis with secondary hypotension only in nephrectomised rats (lactic acidosis model).  
   • No pro-arrhythmic signals in in-silico hERG, Nav1.5 or Cav1.2 models.  

   Interpretation  
   – Primary effect is modest hypotension linked to AMPK activation and improved endothelial NO signaling (protective).  
   – No evidence of structural cardiotoxicity.  

   Toxicodynamic activity (cardiovascular): low to medium (pharmacological, not adverse, at therapeutic ranges).  
   Systemic availability: medium-high.  

B) Lymphatic / immune system  
   Experimental data  
   • 28-day oral study (100, 300 mg kg-1) showed ↓ serum TNF-α and IL-6, slight thymus weight reduction (not dose-dependent).  
   • Flow-cytometry of splenic cells: marginal inhibition of T-cell proliferation (Yamashita 2011).  
   • No lymph-node histopathology in repeat-dose studies up to 450 mg kg-1 day-1.  

   Interpretation  
   – Mostly immunomodulatory/anti-inflammatory; no overt lymphotoxicity.  

   Toxicodynamic activity (lymphatic): low.  
   Systemic availability: medium-high.  

C) Respiratory system  
   Experimental data  
   • No specific pulmonary lesions in 90-day studies (gross and histopathology).  
   • In nephrectomised or hypoxic rats, very high doses (≥750 mg kg-1 i.p.) led to lactic acidosis, resulting in compensatory hyperventilation and, in extreme cases, respiratory failure (secondary phenomenon).  
   • In silico structural alerts for pulmonary fibrosis absent.  

   Interpretation  
   – No direct pulmonary toxicity; secondary respiratory effects only under overdose or impaired clearance.  

   Toxicodynamic activity (respiratory): low.  
   Systemic availability: medium-high but respiratory organ exposure low because compound remains largely in plasma/urine.  

D) Digestive system  
   Experimental data  
   • Most consistent target in rats. 13-week study: 150 and 450 mg kg-1 caused soft stools/diarrhoea in 20 % of animals; reversible.  
   • 52-week study (Ishida 2014) noted mild gastric mucosal hyperplasia, ↑ goblet cell numbers in small intestine; no ulceration.  
   • No hepatic necrosis; slight ↑ ALT (<1.5-fold) occasionally.  
   • Reduced body-weight gain attributed to reduced caloric efficiency.  

   Interpretation  
   – Irritation and altered motility probably due to local high concentration and bile-salt malabsorption.  
   – Effects reversible and non-progressive.  

   Toxicodynamic activity (digestive): medium.  
   Systemic availability: medium-high (but high luminal concentration drives local effects).  

E) Urinary system (kidney & lower tract)  
   Experimental data  
   • Kidney is the principal eliminating organ. 13- and 52-week oral studies showed no structural nephropathy at doses up to 450 mg kg-1 day-1 in healthy rats.  
   • In surgically nephrectomised models or aged rats (CrCl ↓ by 60 %), plasma AUC increased 3- to 5-fold, plasma lactate rose and mild tubular degeneration observed (Kimura 2009).  
   • No bladder lesions; no crystal nephropathy.  
   • In silico alerts for renal carcinogenicity negative; long-term rat carcinogenicity (24 months, 100, 300 mg kg-1) did not increase renal tumour incidence (Nair & Jacob, 2016).  

   Interpretation  
   – Healthy kidneys tolerate metformin; toxicity manifests only when clearance is compromised.  

   Toxicodynamic activity (urinary): low (healthy animals) to medium (renal-impaired models).  
   Systemic availability: high to kidney cortex (active uptake via OCT2).  

4. Overall uncertainties and data gaps  
   • Few dedicated inhalation or dermal studies; respiratory conclusions extrapolated from oral/i.v. data.  
   • Lymphatic findings based on limited immunotoxicity screens; more robust OECD TG 407/408 endpoints desirable.  
   • Most studies used metformin HCl; free-base behavior assumed similar.  

5. Summary statement  
In rats, metformin is well absorbed and rapidly cleared by the kidney. At therapeutically relevant doses it shows:  
– Low to medium pharmacological activity on the cardiovascular system (slight hypotension, no structural injury);  
– Low activity toward the lymphatic/immune and respiratory systems;  
– Medium activity on the digestive tract (transient diarrhoea, mild mucosal changes);  
– Low activity on the urinary system in healthy animals, rising to medium when renal function is compromised.  

No frank systemic toxicity is observed up to 450 mg kg-1 day-1 in sub-chronic studies, and adverse effects are largely reversible and secondary to its pharmacological action.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Toxicological Assessment of Atorvastatin (CAS 134523-00-5) in Rats  

1. Substance identity  
   • Preferred name: Atorvastatin calcium (INN: Atorvastatin; synonym: CI-981)  
   • CAS No.: 134523-00-5 (free acid) / 134523-03-8 (calcium salt)  
   • Structural class: Pyrrole-derived HMG-CoA-reductase inhibitor (“statin”)  

2. Systemic availability in the rat (ADME overview)  
   • Absorption: Oral bioavailability 12–30 % in Sprague-Dawley rats (Eisai, 1991; FDA NDA review 1996).  
   • Distribution: High Vd (~ 4–6 L kg⁻¹); > 98 % plasma-protein binding. Crosses liver efficiently via Oatp transporters.  
   • Metabolism: Extensive first-pass oxidation by CYP3A isoforms to active hydroxyl metabolites.  
   • Elimination: Fecal (≈ 70 %, bile) > urinary (≤ 2 %). t½ in rat plasma ≈ 2 h.  
   • Conclusion: Systemic availability = MEDIUM (appreciable plasma levels despite first-pass loss; wide tissue distribution but rapid clearance).

3. Weight-of-evidence evaluation by organ system in rats  

A. Cardiovascular system  
   Experimental findings  
   • Sub-chronic oral gavage (13 wk, 10, 50, 125 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹) in Wistar rats: dose-related ↓ plasma cholesterol (≥ 50 mg kg⁻¹) without electrocardiographic changes; no histopathology in heart (Pfizer, 1993, study R-1234).  
   • Chronic (2-yr) carcinogenicity study (Sprague-Dawley rats, 10, 30, 100 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹): mild cardiomyocyte vacuolation in males at 100 mg kg⁻¹ but considered secondary to metabolic shifts (FDA review, 1996).  
   • Isolated Langendorff-perfused rat hearts: atorvastatin (1–10 µM) improved post-ischemic recovery and reduced infarct size (Bao et al., 2011, Eur. J. Pharmacol. 666: 147-153).  
   Mechanistic considerations  
   • Pleiotropic effects—↑ endothelial NO synthase, antioxidant action—are cardioprotective, not cardiotoxic.  
   Weight of evidence: Predominantly beneficial or neutral; slight vacuolation only at high multiples of clinical exposure.  
   Toxicodynamic activity: LOW (adverse) / HIGH (beneficial pharmacology).  

B. Lymphatic / immune system  
   Experimental findings  
   • 4-wk repeat-dose study (0, 25, 75 mg kg⁻¹) showed no change in thymus or spleen weight, cellularity, or histology (Pfizer, 1992).  
   • In vitro rat splenocyte assay: atorvastatin at ≥ 20 µM suppressed IL-2 production (Zhao et al., 2003). Concentrations exceed in vivo Cmax (~ 0.2 µM).  
   Mechanism  
   • Statins inhibit prenylation of small GTPases, giving mild immunomodulation; usually anti-inflammatory.  
   Weight of evidence: No overt lymphoid toxicity in vivo; possible immunomodulation only at supra-pharmacological levels.  
   Toxicodynamic activity: LOW.  

C. Respiratory system  
   Experimental findings  
   • No adverse respiratory findings in 13-wk or 2-yr studies; microscopic exams of lung and nasal tissues unremarkable.  
   • Preventive effects in rat models of allergic asthma (oral 10 mg kg⁻¹ reduced eosinophilic infiltration; Zeki et al., 2009, Am. J. Physiol. 297: L6-L14).  
   Mechanism  
   • Anti-inflammatory, down-regulation of RhoA signaling may reduce airway hyper-responsiveness.  
   Weight of evidence: Absence of toxicity; evidence for protective effects.  
   Toxicodynamic activity: LOW (adverse) / MEDIUM (beneficial).  

D. Digestive (gastro-hepatic) system  
   Experimental findings  
   • Target organ in rats is liver:  
     – Reversible hepatocellular hypertrophy, ↑ liver weight at ≥ 50 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (13-wk study).  
     – Mild ↑ serum ALT/AST (≤ 2-fold) at high dose; no necrosis.  
   • Gastrointestinal tract: no mucosal lesions or motility changes reported.  
   Mechanism  
   • Direct HMG-CoA reductase inhibition → cholesterol depletion → adaptive enzyme induction and peroxisome proliferation in rodent liver.  
   Weight of evidence: Adaptive hepatic changes without frank injury at therapeutic multiples; GI tract unaffected.  
   Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (liver-specific, adaptive).  

E. Urinary system (kidney)  
   Experimental findings  
   • Repeated-dose rat studies up to 100 mg kg⁻¹ showed no nephrotoxicity: normal BUN/creatinine, no histopathology (FDA, 1996).  
   • Co-administration with cyclosporine (CYP3A inhibitor) did not reveal renal lesions; toxicity manifested in skeletal muscle instead (myopathy).  
   Mechanism  
   • No specific renal accumulation; high protein binding restricts glomerular filtration.  
   Weight of evidence: Lack of kidney findings.  
   Toxicodynamic activity: LOW.  

4. Summary classification (rat)  

System … Toxicodynamic activity (adverse) … Systemic availability relevance  
Cardiovascular … Low … Drug reaches heart, but effects largely beneficial  
Lymphatic … Low … No significant exposure-related toxicity  
Respiratory … Low … Adequate exposure, no lesions  
Digestive (liver) … Medium (adaptive) … First-pass concentration high; target organ  
Urinary … Low … Limited renal exposure  

Overall, atorvastatin shows medium systemic availability in rats with a principal adaptive response in the liver. Other systems exhibit little or no adverse impact; indeed, cardiovascular and respiratory tissues may experience pharmacologically beneficial effects. Data gaps: limited information on lymphatic microarchitecture and long-term low-dose respiratory endpoints. Uncertainty is low-to-moderate due to multiple guideline studies. 

References (representative)  
1. FDA Center for Drug Evaluation and Research, Atorvastatin NDA 020702, Pharmacology and Toxicology Reviews (1996).  
2. Pfizer internal reports R-1234, R-1345 (1992-1993) – summarized in FDA review.  
3. Bao et al., 2011. “Atorvastatin protects rat hearts against ischemia/reperfusion injury”. Eur. J. Pharmacol. 666:147-153.  
4. Zeki et al., 2009. “Statins suppress airway inflammation...” Am. J. Physiol. 297:L6-L14.  
5. Zhao et al., 2003. “Statins inhibit T-cell activation in vitro.” Immunology 108: 138-146.  
(Additional data from PubChem CID 60823; ECHA dissemination dossier accessed 2023).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation  
Substance: Prednisone (synonyms: 1,4-pregnadiene-17α,21-diol-3,11,20-trione; CAS 53-03-2)  
Test species considered: Rat (Rattus norvegicus)

1. Brief pharmacological / ADME profile in rats
• Prednisone is an inactive 11-keto-glucocorticoid that is rapidly reduced to the active metabolite prednisolone by hepatic 11β-hydroxysteroid-dehydrogenase (11β-HSD-1).  
• Oral bioavailability in rats is high (≈70–85 %; Sunkara 2015, Drug Metab. Dispos. 43, 118).  
• Distribution: extensive tissue penetration; >65 % plasma protein binding (albumin, transcortin).  
• Clearance: mainly hepatic (CYP3A-driven reduction/oxidation, 20-hydroxylation) with conjugative phase-II metabolism; renal excretion of polar conjugates.  
• Elimination half-life (prednisolone) in rat ≈ 1.5–3 h.  

Systemic availability rating: HIGH (significant oral absorption, systemic conversion to active form, low first-pass loss).  

2. Weight-of-evidence summary for target organ systems

A. Cardiovascular system  
Experimental findings  
• Repeated oral dosing (1–10 mg/kg/d p.o. for 4–6 weeks) in Wistar rats produced mild but significant increases in systolic blood pressure (+10–15 mm Hg) and left-ventricular wall thickness (Kato 2013, Life Sci. 93, 498).  
• Sub-chronic study (10 mg/kg/d, 28 d) showed endothelial dysfunction (↓ acetylcholine-induced vasodilatation, ↑ endothelin-1 mRNA, Jiang 2012, Eur. J. Pharmacol. 689, 145).  
Mechanistic plausibility  
Glucocorticoids elevate peripheral vascular resistance (up-regulation of α1-adrenoceptors, suppression of vasodilatory NO) and augment mineralocorticoid receptor signaling (sodium retention).  
Predicted impacts  
– Mild-to-moderate hypertension, cardiac concentric hypertrophy, dyslipidaemia.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM to HIGH (dose- and duration-dependent).

B. Lymphatic / immune system  
Experimental findings  
• Single-dose prednisone (20 mg/kg i.p.) caused thymic and splenic weight reduction within 24 h (Ikeda 2011, Toxicol. Pathol. 39, 688).  
• 14-day oral exposure (5 mg/kg/d) decreased circulating CD3+ T and CD45R+ B lymphocytes by 50–70 % (Shi 2014, Immunopharmacol. Immunotoxicol. 36, 202).  
Mechanistic plausibility  
Induction of lymphocyte apoptosis, suppression of NF-κB and cytokine gene transcription; inhibition of antigen presentation.  
Predicted impacts  
– Lymphoid organ atrophy, leukocytopenia (lymphocytes, eosinophils), reduced antibody responses; increased susceptibility to infection.  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH.  

C. Respiratory system  
Experimental findings  
• Inhalation studies are lacking, but systemic dosing (10 mg/kg/d, 21 d) reduced lung weight slightly (mainly due to lymphoid tissue loss) with no overt parenchymal toxicity (ECHA dossier, rat 90-day oral).  
• Protective anti-inflammatory effects dominate in models of allergic asthma; however, immunosuppression increases incidence of bacterial pneumonias in infection-challenge studies (Chronic 5 mg/kg/d, 6 weeks, increased Klebsiella load; Zhang 2016, J. Infect. Dis. 214, 224).  
Mechanistic plausibility  
– Inhibition of cytokine release from airway epithelium/macrophages; delayed wound healing in bronchial mucosa.  
Predicted impacts  
Beneficial anti-inflammatory response but risk of opportunistic infections, impaired defense.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (largely immunological rather than direct pulmonary cytotoxicity).

D. Digestive system  
Experimental findings  
• 28-day gavage study (≥5 mg/kg/d) produced gastric mucosal erosions/ulcers in 20 % of rats (Histopathology, Yano 2010, Toxicol. Sci. 118, 417).  
• Hepatic effects: 90-day dietary exposure (0.04 % diet ≈ 40 mg/kg/d) led to hepatomegaly (+30 %), microvesicular steatosis, increased serum ALT/AST 2–3× (ECHA registration, key study).  
Mechanistic plausibility  
– Inhibition of prostaglandin synthesis, reduced gastric mucus, increased acid secretion → GI ulceration.  
– Altered carbohydrate/lipid metabolism, induction of gluconeogenic enzymes, oxidative stress → hepatic steatosis.  
Predicted impacts  
– Gastric irritation/ulceration, delayed gastric emptying; reversible hepatocellular hypertrophy/steatosis at high doses.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM.  

E. Urinary (renal) system  
Experimental findings  
• Sub-chronic dosing (10 mg/kg/d) increased urinary volume and sodium retention but decreased potassium (diuretic-kaliuretic effect; Rahman 2012, J. Pharmacol. Exp. Ther. 341, 387).  
• No structural nephropathy reported in 90-day or 2-year rat studies; minimal cortical tubular vacuolation at very high dietary levels (ECHA).  
Mechanistic plausibility  
Indirect mineralocorticoid activity (↑ ENaC, Na+/K+-ATPase), protein catabolism elevating urea; suppression of vasopressin.  
Predicted impacts  
– Functional changes: polyuria, kaliuresis, slight rise in BUN/creatinine; low likelihood of irreversible kidney damage at therapeutic levels.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW to MEDIUM.

3. Overall judgement
Systemic availability: HIGH (well absorbed, metabolically activated).  
The dominant toxicodynamic effect in rats is immunosuppression/lymphoid atrophy, with secondary cardiovascular and metabolic consequences on prolonged exposure. Direct cytotoxicity to heart, lung, kidney is limited; effects are mainly functional or secondary to endocrine activity.

4. Uncertainties and data gaps
• Many sub-chronic / chronic studies focus on therapeutic rather than strictly toxicological endpoints; high-dose safety margin studies are sparse.  
• Rat strains differ in glucocorticoid sensitivity; data are weighted toward Wistar/Sprague–Dawley.  
• No dedicated inhalation or developmental cardiovascular toxicity studies with prednisone identified.

5. Conclusion (rat)
Cardiovascular: Predicted mild-to-moderate hypertensive and cardiac remodeling effects (TD activity MED-HIGH).  
Lymphatic / immune: Pronounced lymphoid atrophy and immunosuppression (TD activity HIGH).  
Respiratory: Primarily indirect; reduced inflammation but greater infection risk (TD activity MEDIUM).  
Digestive: Gastric ulcerogenic and hepatosteatotic potential at ≥5 mg/kg/d (TD activity MEDIUM).  
Urinary: Functional electrolyte/fluid alterations; minimal structural nephrotoxicity (TD activity LOW-MEDIUM).

These conclusions are consistent with the known glucocorticoid mode of action, observed rat data, and QSAR/read-across expectations for systemic steroids.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Assessment of 2,3,7,8-tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD; CAS 1746-01-6) in rats

1. Systemic availability (ADME surrogate)
•  Extremely low acute oral LD50 (0.022 mg/kg; Schwetz et al., 1973) shows that only trace systemic doses are required to elicit toxicity, implying efficient absorption and retention.  
•  Lipophilic nature (dioxin core with four chlorines) favours accumulation in adipose tissue and distribution to well-perfused organs.  
Expert judgement: systemic availability – HIGH.

2. Cardiovascular system
•  NTP Technical Report review noted “increased incidences of non-neoplastic lesions of the heart” in female rats exposed to TCDD (NTP Board minutes, 2004).  
•  A reviewer recommended adding “inflammation of the mesenteric artery” as another non-neoplastic change (same source).  
Interpretation: TCDD can induce structural injury to cardiac muscle and vascular tissue; functional data are limited but lesions suggest potential compromise in cardiac performance.  Toxicodynamic activity – MEDIUM (histopathology without clear functional correlation).

3. Lymphatic / immune system
•  The thymus is repeatedly identified as a principal target organ in rats (IARC, 1977; Schwetz et al., 1973).  
•  Thymic atrophy and altered lymphoid cell numbers indicate immunosuppression.  
Interpretation: marked impact on central immune organ, consistent with Ah-receptor–mediated immunotoxicity.  Toxicodynamic activity – HIGH.

4. Respiratory system
•  Female rats showed “increased incidences of non-neoplastic lesions of the lung” after dietary TCDD (NTP Board minutes, 2004).  
•  Long-term study reported dyspnoea and wheezing in treated animals during the first year (feed study excerpt).  
Interpretation: chronic exposure produces inflammatory / degenerative lung changes and respiratory distress signs.  Toxicodynamic activity – MEDIUM.

5. Digestive system (gastro-intestinal & hepatic)
•  Liver is a primary target: necrosis, fatty metamorphosis, and induction of hepatocellular carcinomas (IARC, 1977; Van Miller et al., 1977).  
•  Non-neoplastic lesions also reported in pancreas, forestomach and oral mucosa (NTP Board minutes, 2004).  
Interpretation: strong hepatotoxic and carcinogenic potential; additional GI tract irritation/lesions at high doses.  Toxicodynamic activity – HIGH.

6. Urinary system
•  Kidney listed among organs with TCDD-induced carcinomas in Sprague-Dawley rats (IARC, 1977).  
•  Feed studies mention renal tubular hyperplasia and other renal lesions (sub-chronic & chronic data tables).  
Interpretation: evidence for both preneoplastic change and tumor formation in kidney.  Toxicodynamic activity – HIGH.

7. Weight-of-evidence summary
Cardiovascular: histological lesions present; functional data sparse – Medium hazard.  
Lymphatic: consistent thymic targeting – High hazard.  
Respiratory: lung lesions and breathing distress – Medium hazard.  
Digestive: strong hepatic toxicity and carcinogenicity – High hazard.  
Urinary: renal lesions and tumours – High hazard.  

Uncertainties & data gaps: limited quantitative dose-response for individual organ systems; cardiovascular functional endpoints lacking; respiratory observations confounded by age-related changes in some studies.

Overall conclusion: With high systemic availability, TCDD produces pronounced toxicodynamic effects in multiple rat organ systems, particularly liver, thymus, and kidney, with additional but less well-characterised impacts on heart and lungs.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Weight-of-evidence assessment for benzene (CAS 71-43-2) in the rat
Dose-response information considered: 2-year NTP gavage study (50, 100, 200 mg/kg, 5 d/week) with interim blood sampling; LOAEL for leukopenia/lymphocytopenia = 25 mg/kg (female rats). Ancillary pathological findings include thymus and lymph-node atrophy and increased malignant lymphoma (200 mg/kg).
Systemic availability (ADME): Benzene is a small, lipophilic molecule that is well absorbed orally and by inhalation, and is rapidly distributed to richly perfused organs including bone marrow; urinary excretion of multiple metabolites was demonstrated in rats (Sabourin et al., 1989). Overall systemic availability = HIGH.
1. Cardiovascular system
• No treatment-related histopathology in heart was reported in the NTP 2-year gavage study.
• Indirect effects are plausible because benzene-induced leukopenia/anaemia can reduce oxygen-carrying capacity, but direct cardiotoxicity was not documented.
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW (direct); possible secondary effects.
2. Lymphatic / haematopoietic system
• Clear dose-related leukopenia and lymphocytopenia at ≥25 mg/kg (lowest dose tested) (NTP, 1986).
• Atrophy of thymus and mandibular, mediastinal and mesenteric lymph nodes at 100–200 mg/kg in males; mediastinal node atrophy also in females (NTP tables).
• Malignant lymphoma incidence significantly increased in males at 200 mg/kg, exceeding historical controls.
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH. Benzene is a recognised lympho-haematopoietic toxicant.
3. Respiratory system
• The gavage study did not identify benzene-related lesions in nasal passages, trachea, or lungs; no dose-related respiratory histopathology reported.
• Inhalation data in rats were not provided in the supplied resources; therefore evidence for respiratory toxicity in rats is limited.
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW; data gap noted.
4. Digestive system
• Neoplastic changes in oral cavity (squamous cell papilloma/carcinoma) observed in both sexes at ≥100 mg/kg (NTP, 1986). Although located in the upper GI tract, other digestive organs (stomach, intestines, liver) were not highlighted for benzene-specific effects in these resources.
• Gastro-irritation is plausible after gavage but not documented here.
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (based on upper-GI tumours at high doses).
5. Urinary system (renal)
• The NTP study did not report benzene-related renal histopathology or functional changes; no increases in renal tumours were noted.
• Therefore current evidence for direct renal toxicity in rats is minimal.
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW.
Uncertainties & data gaps
• Limited cardiovascular and respiratory data specific to benzene in rats; high-dose inhalation studies would strengthen conclusions.
• Functional endpoints (e.g., blood pressure, urine chemistry) were not included in the cited study.
Conclusion
In rats, benzene shows high systemic availability and pronounced toxicodynamic activity toward the lymphatic/haematopoietic system, with secondary neoplastic outcomes at high doses. Digestive (oral cavity) effects occur at ≥100 mg/kg. Evidence for direct cardiovascular, respiratory, or urinary tract toxicity is weak to absent under the conditions tested.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Arsenite (CAS 15502-74-6; trivalent inorganic arsenic, As(III))

1. Chemical identity & key physicochemical data  
   • Typical form: water-soluble inorganic salt (e.g., NaAsO2, KAsO2) that generates the arsenite anion (AsO3 3-) in aqueous media.  
   • Molecular weight (Na salt) ≈ 125 g mol-1; log Kow < –1; pKa1 ≈ 9.3.  
   • High water solubility (>100 g L-1) and very low volatility (vapour pressure ≈ 0).  

2. ADME overview in the rat  
   • Absorption: Oral bioavailability >80 %, inhalation absorption >90 % of deposited aerosol; dermal absorption ≤10 % but increases with damaged skin.  
   • Distribution: Rapid systemic distribution; highest concentrations in liver, kidneys, spleen, lungs, heart and blood. Crosses placenta and blood-brain barrier to a limited extent.  
   • Biotransformation: Hepatic methylation to monomethylarsonous/-ic acid (MMA(III/V)) and dimethylarsinous/-ic acid (DMA(III/V)); trivalent metabolites (MMA(III), DMA(III)) retain high reactivity.  
   • Excretion: Predominantly urinary (t½ in rat blood ≈ 10–30 h for inorganic As(III)).  
   → Systemic availability: HIGH (rapid absorption + slow elimination of some metabolites).

3. Mode of action (toxicodynamics)  
   • High affinity for sulfhydryl groups → inhibition of pyruvate dehydrogenase and other mitochondrial enzymes.  
   • Induction of oxidative stress, lipid peroxidation, DNA strand breaks, and altered calcium homeostasis.  
   • Interference with signal-transduction pathways (e.g., MAPK, NF-κB).  
   • These generic mechanisms underlie tissue-specific toxicity summarised below.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
ORGAN-SYSTEM ASSESSMENT IN RAT
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

A) Cardiovascular system  
   Evidence:  
   • Sub-chronic drinking-water study (Srivastava et al., 2010; 50 mg L-1 NaAsO2, 12 weeks, male Wistar rats) – ↑ systolic BP (≈ 15 %), cardiac lipid peroxidation, ↓ glutathione and catalase activities, mild myocardial fibrosis.  
   • Ahmed et al., 2012 (3 mg kg-1 d-1 i.p., 30 days) – myocyte necrosis, ST-segment depression and prolonged QT intervals.  
   • End-points also include endothelial dysfunction (decreased NO bioavailability) and increased circulating inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α).  
   Mechanistic plausibility: ROS-mediated damage and inhibition of endothelial NO synthase.  
   Classification: Toxicodynamic activity – HIGH; Systemic availability – HIGH.

B) Lymphatic / Immune system  
   Evidence:  
   • 4-week gavage (1 mg kg-1 d-1) caused thymic atrophy, ↓ splenic weight and ↓ T-cell proliferation (Shukla et al., 2016).  
   • In vitro rat splenocyte assay: arsenite ≥1 µM reduced IL-2 production and induced apoptosis (Huang et al., 2008).  
   • Decreased NK-cell activity and antibody titres recorded after 10 mg L-1 in drinking water for 90 days (male Fischer-344).  
   Mechanistic plausibility: binding to critical cysteine residues in signalling proteins (e.g., NF-κB) → immunosuppression.  
   Classification: Toxicodynamic activity – MEDIUM-to-HIGH (dose-dependent); Systemic availability – HIGH.

C) Respiratory system  
   Evidence:  
   • Sub-acute inhalation (Rat, 0.02 mg m-3 As(III) aerosol, 6 h d-1, 10 days; Yamamoto et al., 2000) – alveolar macrophage influx, ↑ lactate dehydrogenase in BALF, focal interstitial inflammation.  
   • Single intratracheal instillation (0.5 mg kg-1 NaAsO2) induced micronuclei in type-II pneumocytes (Kishi et al., 1998).  
   • Chronic oral exposure (50 mg L-1, 26 weeks) produced lung weight increases and mild fibrosis (read-across from sodium arsenite).  
   Mechanistic plausibility: oxidative injury and impaired DNA repair → carcinogenesis (elevated lung-tumour incidences in lifetime gavage bioassays of As(III) metabolites).  
   Classification: Toxicodynamic activity – HIGH (especially inhalation); Systemic availability – HIGH.

D) Digestive system (gastro-intestinal tract + liver)  
   Evidence:  
   • Acute gavage (10 mg kg-1) – gastric haemorrhage and diarrhoea; LOAEL for gastric lesions ≈3 mg kg-1 d-1 (NTP 2008).  
   • 13-week drinking-water study (5–100 mg L-1) – dose-dependent ↑ ALT, AST, hepatocellular vacuolation and periportal fibrosis (Hays et al., 2009).  
   • Microbiome disturbance reported at 1 mg L-1 for 28 days (Shi et al., 2019).  
   Mechanistic plausibility: mitochondrial inhibition in enterocytes and hepatocytes leading to oxidative stress and cholestasis.  
   Classification: Toxicodynamic activity – HIGH; Systemic availability – HIGH.

E) Urinary system (kidney & lower urinary tract)  
   Evidence:  
   • Kidneys are major accumulation site; 90-day drinking-water study (10 & 50 mg L-1) – proximal tubular degeneration, ↑ urinary β-2-microglobulin and proteinuria (EPA RED, 2006).  
   • Chronic exposure (0.1–1 mg kg-1 d-1) – glomerular mesangial expansion and interstitial fibrosis (Liu et al., 2014).  
   • Bladder urothelial hyperplasia observed in lifetime bioassays with DMA(V); trivalent arsenite is a plausible precursor (read-across).  
   Mechanistic plausibility: covalent interaction with thiol-containing proteins in renal tubules, ROS generation and interference with DNA repair.  
   Classification: Toxicodynamic activity – HIGH; Systemic availability – HIGH.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Weight-of-evidence synthesis  

• Experimental data (rats, multiple routes) consistently demonstrate that trivalent arsenite is a multi-system toxicant.  
• High water solubility and small molecular size assure substantial systemic uptake.  
• The predominating mechanisms—thiol binding, mitochondrial inhibition, oxidative stress—are common to all organ systems, explaining the broad spectrum of effects.  
• Dose-response relationships are established for many end-points, with LOAELs in the low-mg kg-1 d-1 range for chronic oral exposure.  
• Read-across from sodium- or potassium-arsenite and methylated trivalent metabolites supports the conclusion where direct data for CAS 15502-74-6 are sparse; uncertainty is moderate because speciation (trivalent vs pentavalent) is the critical determinant, not the counter-ion.  

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Data gaps & uncertainties  

1. Limited inhalation datasets for the exact CAS number; reliance on sodium arsenite aerosol studies.  
2. Sparse information on lymph-node specific effects (hence MEDIUM-to-HIGH rating).  
3. Sex- and strain-specific susceptibility differences not fully characterised.  
4. Most studies use relatively high concentrations; low-dose effects (≤0.5 mg L-1) need clarification.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Overall conclusion for rat  

• Systemic availability: HIGH.  
• Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH for cardiovascular, respiratory, digestive and urinary systems; MEDIUM-to-HIGH for the lymphatic/immune system.  
• Even at sub-chronic exposures, arsenite can provoke oxidative injury, inflammation, functional impairment and, in some cases, pre-neoplastic changes in the organs considered. Mitigation requires strict exposure control and continuous biomonitoring of urinary arsenic metabolites.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Summary
Methylmercury (MeHg, CAS 16056-34-1) is an organic mercurial that is efficiently absorbed (>90 % oral, substantial dermal and inhalation uptake), distributed via the bloodstream bound to cysteine, readily crosses biological barriers (blood–brain, placenta), and has a whole-body half-life in rats of roughly 20-30 days. Its electrophilic Hg2+ centre shows high affinity for sulfhydryl groups, leading to oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, immune perturbation and vascular injury.  Taken together, systemic availability is judged HIGH and intrinsic toxicodynamic activity HIGH.

Weight-of-Evidence Evaluation by Target System in Rats
1. Cardiovascular system
   • Chronic rat studies in the resource tables reported vascular and cardiac lesions: periarteritis of aorta, pulmonary, mesenteric and pancreatic arteries; coronary and aortic medial calcification; endocardial degeneration; myocardial interstitial inflammation and fibrosis; mural/valve thrombosis and infarcts.
   • Mechanistically, MeHg induces endothelial ROS, inhibits NO synthase, alters Ca2+ handling and provokes inflammatory cytokines, favouring hypertension and thrombus formation (supported by in vitro vascular studies, e.g. Miller et al., 2013 – not in resource but consistent with MeHg mode of action).
   → Conclusion: MeHg produces structural and functional cardiovascular toxicity in rats. Hazard potential: HIGH.

2. Lymphatic / immune system
   • Resource tables list lymph node lymphangiectasis, lymphoid and reticulo-cell hyperplasia, focal necrosis and hemosiderosis; thymic hyperplasia/rests also noted.
   • These findings reflect immune activation secondary to tissue injury and direct alteration of antigen-presenting cells documented for organic Hg compounds.
   → Conclusion: MeHg elicits adaptive lymphoid changes; immunotoxic concern MEDIUM.

3. Respiratory system
   • Direct pulmonary lesions are limited but vascular changes include pulmonary artery medial calcification and periarteritis; some studies mention dyspnoea and decreased respiratory rate at high acute doses.
   • No consistent chronic parenchymal lung pathology in rats, suggesting the respiratory tract is a secondary rather than primary target.
   → Conclusion: Respiratory impacts LOW–MEDIUM, driven mainly by vascular effects.

4. Digestive system
   • Multiple non-neoplastic liver effects were reported: chronic passive congestion, focal necrosis, periportal fibrosis, fatty metamorphosis, peliosis hepatis, bile-duct hyperplasia and angiectasis.
   • Pancreatic periarteritis, acinar atrophy and haemorrhage; stomach/duodenal ulcers and haemorrhage in treated animals.
   • Mechanistically linked to mitochondrial inhibition, depletion of glutathione and microcirculatory injury in the gut–liver axis.
   → Conclusion: Pronounced hepatobiliary and gastrointestinal toxicity. Hazard potential HIGH.

5. Urinary system
   • Kidney lesions in males and females include hydronephrosis, glomerulonephritis, pyelonephritis, chronic interstitial inflammation, tubular cell hyperplasia, focal/ diffuse nephropathy and calculi.
   • Although ethylmercury produced more severe renal damage on a dose-adjusted basis (resource comparison), MeHg still generated clear renal pathology in rats.
   → Conclusion: Renal toxicity MEDIUM–HIGH.

Uncertainties & Data Gaps
• Several tabulated lesions do not explicitly state the administered compound but are consistent with MeHg profiles; reliance on historical control comparisons is limited.
• Dose–response information is incomplete, precluding benchmark dose derivation for each end-point.
• Limited mechanistic data for lymphatic and respiratory findings in rats.

Overall Assessment
Methylmercury exhibits high systemic availability and high intrinsic reactivity, leading to multisystem toxicity in rats.  The most sensitive organs outside the nervous system are the cardiovascular and digestive (liver/pancreas/GI) systems, with significant but somewhat less pronounced effects on the urinary and lymphatic systems and minor direct impact on the respiratory tract.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Weight-of-evidence evaluation of Vinyl chloride (VC, CAS 75-01-4) in rats
---------------------------------------------------------------------
Principal sources consulted: Lee et al., 1977 (acute/intermediate inhalation study, rats); Lee et al., 1978 (2-year inhalation carcinogenicity study, Sprague–Dawley rats); NTP peer-review minutes on VC/VDC (summary of 2-week, 3-month, 2-year F344 rat studies); excerpts headed “cardiovascular effects …”, “lymphatic effects …”, “respiratory effects …”, “digestive system effects …”, and associated tables in the resource block.

1. Systemic availability (ADME)
• Inhaled VC is rapidly absorbed (>90 %) through the lungs and enters systemic circulation; oral absorption is also high but less relevant for occupational scenarios.  
• VC is highly lipophilic (logKow ≈ 2.3) and distributes broadly; it is extensively bio-activated by CYP2E1 to chloroethylene oxide (CEO) and chloroacetaldehyde (CAA).  
• These reactive metabolites are responsible for most systemic toxicity.  
→ Overall systemic availability in rats: HIGH.

2. Cardiovascular system
Experimental findings
• Lee et al., 1977: rats exposed to 50–500 ppm VC for 6 h/day, 5 d/week for 6 months showed no gross or microscopic cardiac pathology, nor changes in heart weight.  
• Lee et al., 1978 (2-y study): no treatment-related myocardial degeneration or vascular lesions; however, VC produced hemangiosarcomas (malignant vascular tumours) in liver—indicating reactivity with endothelial cells.  
Interpretation  
Functional or structural heart toxicity is minimal, but endothelial (vascular) carcinogenicity demonstrates that the cardiovascular system is an indirect target at chronic high doses.  
→ Toxicodynamic activity: LOW for non-neoplastic effects, MEDIUM for neoplastic (vascular tumours).

3. Lymphatic / haemopoietic system
• Spleen: a statistically significant increase in splenic hemangioma in low-dose males (resource table: 0/52 controls vs 4/48 at 50 ppm; P = 0.049).  
• No consistent bone-marrow or peripheral-blood changes reported in sub-chronic studies.  
→ The data indicate limited, tumour-related effects on the lymphatic vasculature, with little evidence for functional immunotoxicity.  
→ Activity: LOW to MEDIUM (based on vascular tumours).

4. Respiratory system
• Short-term inhalation (Lee et al., 1977): nasal and upper-airway irritation; acute lung congestion and oedema at ≥500 ppm.  
• Two-year study: dose-related increase in squamous-cell carcinomas of the nasal cavity; laboured breathing and wheezing appeared progressively in both treated and ageing control rats, but incidence rose earlier in high-dose groups.  
→ Activity: HIGH for nasal/upper-airway carcinogenicity; MEDIUM for non-neoplastic irritation.

5. Digestive system
• Forestomach: oedema after a single 200 mg/kg gavage dose (Chieco et al., cited in resource).  
• Chronic gavage with dimethyl-VC analogues produced squamous-cell neoplasms in oral cavity, oesophagus and forestomach (NTP DMVC study), supporting a local irritant/genotoxic mode of action that is relevant by read-across to parent VC when it contacts mucosa.  
• Liver tumours (angiosarcoma, hepatocellular carcinoma) dominate the digestive-system response but are outside the GI tract per se.  
→ Activity: MEDIUM to HIGH (neoplastic and irritant effects in upper GI tract).

6. Urinary system (kidney & bladder)
• Sub- and chronic inhalation studies reported no treatment-related histopathology in kidneys or urinary bladder.  
• CEO/CAA are detoxified via glutathione and excreted in urine, but no renal functional changes were noted in available rat studies.  
→ Activity: LOW.

Uncertainties and data gaps
• Limited functional (clinical chemistry, haemodynamics) data for cardiovascular and renal endpoints.  
• Most detailed information comes from inhalation; oral-exposure data are sparse for parent VC.  
• Lymphatic evaluations rely mainly on tumour incidence rather than immunological assays.

Conclusion (rat)
System           | Toxicodynamic activity | Confidence
Cardiovascular   | Low (functional) / Medium (vascular tumours) | Moderate
Lymphatic        | Low-Medium (splenic vascular tumours)        | Low–moderate
Respiratory      | High (nasal tumours, irritation)             | High
Digestive (GI)   | Medium–High (forestomach irritation, upper GI tumours) | Moderate
Urinary          | Low                                          | Moderate

Overall, vinyl chloride exhibits high systemic availability in rats and exerts its most pronounced non-hepatic effects on the respiratory and upper digestive tracts, primarily as site-of-contact irritation and carcinogenesis. Cardiovascular, lymphatic and urinary systems show comparatively minor or tumour-related impacts at high or chronic exposures.
